heading: One But Not Both
body: **Exclusive or** means exactly one of two statements is true. It allows the left statement alone or the right statement alone, but it rejects the case where both are true.

examples.text: "With dinner, may have soup or salad" can mean one choice, not both.
"The porch light is on or the garage light is on" is exclusive in every context.

note: Context must show that "or" excludes the both-true case.

heading: Context Shows Exclusivity
body: English "or" is not always exclusive. Choice rules, menus, and either-one-only instructions often signal exclusivity. Ordinary factual claims often do not.

examples.text: "Choose tea or coffee" often means one drink only.
"The package is in the hall or the kitchen" does not usually mean exactly one by rule.

heading: Translation Pattern
body: The **exclusive disjunction form** is $(P \vee Q) \wedge \sim (P \wedge Q)$. The first part allows at least one. The second part blocks both together.

examples.text: "$P$ or $Q$, but not both" becomes $(P \vee Q) \wedge \sim (P \wedge Q)$.
"$P$ or $Q$, but not both" becomes $P \vee Q$.

heading: Meal Choice Example
body: Translate the ordinary disjunction first. Then add the negated conjunction to exclude the both-true case.

examples.text: Let $S$ = Have soup, $L$ = Have salad. "With dinner, may have soup or salad" becomes $(S \vee L) \wedge \sim (S \wedge L)$.
Let $T$ = Have tea, $C$ = Have coffee. "Choose tea or coffee" becomes $(T \vee C) \wedge \sim (T \wedge C)$.

heading: Plain Or Is Too Weak
body: A common mistake is translating exclusive or as a plain disjunction. But $P \vee Q$ still allows both $P$ and $Q$ to be true together.

examples.text: $P \vee Q$ is enough for exclusive or.
$(P \vee Q) \wedge \sim (P \wedge Q)$ excludes the both-true case.

heading: Core Pattern
body: Use exclusive or only when context means one or the other, but not both. Translate it as $(P \vee Q) \wedge \sim (P \wedge Q)$.
